items.correct: The change from one additional unit — marginal cost is the cost of producing one more unit; marginal utility is the satisfaction from one more unit. Marginal is incremental, not total or average.
items.misconception: Marginal cost is the total cost of producing all units.
items.why: 'Marginal' has a specific technical meaning: the next unit. Profit maximisation, optimal consumption and many other results depend on marginal — not total or average — quantities. Students conflate the three measures.

items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs don't change with output (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change directly with output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs never change at all, ever.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are paid once and variable costs are paid daily.
items.why: Fixed costs are fixed WITH RESPECT TO OUTPUT — they can change for other reasons (rent goes up). The distinction is whether producing more or less changes the cost.

items.q: What determines the size of the fiscal multiplier?
items.correct: Mainly the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) — the higher the share of extra income people spend domestically, the bigger the multiplier. Leakages (saving, imports, taxes) reduce the multiplier.
items.misconception: The fiscal multiplier is always 2 or 3.
items.random_wrong: The fiscal multiplier is the rate of growth of the money supply.
items.why: The multiplier varies with conditions — bigger in slumps with high MPC and slack capacity, smaller at full employment or in open economies. Students learn a fixed number rather than the determining mechanism.
Correct answer: Swapping goods directly. “Saving money” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Money as a store of value means…
items.why: Correct answer: You can spend it later. “You print it” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many functions of money were listed? (4 ‘jobs’)
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
